submitting__lead_agency_information.from_public_agency: City of Santa Rosa Planning and Economic Development Department

project_information.project_title: Roseland Community Pocket Park Improvements Project
project_information.project_applicant: City of Santa Rosa, Transportation and Public Works Department
project_information.description_of_nature_purpose_and_benefi:
The Roseland Community Pocket Park Improvements Project consists of minor improvements and upgrades to an existing 0.8-acre neighborhood pocket park located at 665 Sebastopol Road in the Roseland area of Santa Rosa. The park is already developed with turf, a small play area, and a few benches. The project will replace aging playground equipment with new, ADA-compliant play structures, install new resilient safety surfacing, upgrade existing lighting to energy-efficient LED fixtures, replace and widen existing pathways to meet current ADA accessibility standards, add two small shade structures with picnic tables, refurbish existing landscaping with drought-tolerant plantings, and install a small bioswale feature to improve stormwater management within the site footprint.

The primary purpose of the project is to maintain, repair, and modestly enhance an existing public recreational facility to improve safety, accessibility, and usability for local residents, including children, seniors, and individuals with disabilities, without expanding the park’s footprint or intensifying its use beyond what currently exists. The project does not involve any increase in park acreage, no new parking lots, no new vehicle access points, and no expansion of operating hours. Construction activities will be limited to the already disturbed and landscaped park area.

Beneficiaries of the project include families living within walking distance in the Roseland neighborhood—an area with a high proportion of working-class and immigrant households—as well as nearby students who use the park after school, seniors from adjacent multifamily housing, and local community groups that occasionally hold small gatherings at the site. The improvements are intended to make the park safer (by replacing outdated equipment and improving lighting), more inclusive (by providing ADA-compliant features and accessible seating), and more environmentally resilient (through drought-tolerant landscaping and on-site stormwater treatment), thereby contributing to neighborhood quality of life and public health without generating significant new environmental impacts.
project_information.name_of_public_agency_approving_project: City of Santa Rosa City Council
project_information.name_of_person_or_agency_carrying_out_pr: City of Santa Rosa, Transportation and Public Works Department – Capital Projects Engineering Division

exemption_status.categorical_exemption_state_type_and_sec: Class 1, Existing Facilities (CEQA Guidelines § 15301); Class 3, New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures (CEQA Guidelines § 15303); and Class 4, Minor Alterations to Land (CEQA Guidelines § 15304)
exemption_status.reasons_why_project_is_exempt:
The Roseland Community Pocket Park Improvements Project is exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to the following categorical exemptions under the State CEQA Guidelines: (1) Class 1, Existing Facilities (Section 15301); (2) Class 3, New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures (Section 15303); and (3) Class 4, Minor Alterations to Land (Section 15304).

Class 1 (Existing Facilities – § 15301): The project involves the minor alteration of an existing public facility with no expansion of use or increase in capacity. The pocket park is already developed and currently functions as an active neighborhood park with turf, a small play area, and benches. The proposed work—replacement of existing playground equipment, resurfacing of existing pathways, upgrading of existing lighting fixtures, and repair of worn site furnishings—constitutes repair, maintenance, and minor alteration of existing public facilities. The project does not increase the park’s size, the number of access points, or intensify its use beyond the existing condition. All work will occur within the park’s existing boundaries and previously disturbed areas.

Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures – § 15303): The installation of two small shade structures with integrated picnic tables and a small prefabricated storage cabinet for parks maintenance staff qualifies as the construction of small accessory structures under Class 3. These structures are modest in scale, typical of neighborhood parks, and are subordinate to the primary recreational use of the site. No new buildings for occupancy are proposed, and the small structures will not require significant utility extensions beyond short connections to the existing electrical service for lighting.

Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land – § 15304): The project includes minor landscaping improvements such as planting drought-tolerant shrubs and trees, installing mulch, and creating a small bioswale within an already landscaped area to improve on-site stormwater infiltration. These activities involve minor alterations to land that do not involve the removal of healthy, mature, scenic trees and are limited to previously disturbed and maintained park grounds. Grading is limited to shallow recontouring within the park to direct runoff into the bioswale and to achieve accessible path slopes, and will not result in significant changes in topography.

No exceptions to the use of these categorical exemptions, as set forth in CEQA Guidelines Section 15300.2, apply to this project. The project is not located on a particularly sensitive site such as a designated state scenic highway, hazardous waste site, or officially designated historical resource. The park is in an urbanized area surrounded by existing residential and commercial development, and the site is not in or adjacent to any riparian habitat, wetlands, or other sensitive biological resources. There are no known special-status species on the site, and all work will occur within existing landscaped and previously disturbed areas.

The project will not result in cumulative impacts of the same type and in the same place as other projects because it consists solely of minor repairs and upgrades to an existing neighborhood park and does not induce growth or change land use designations. There are no unusual circumstances that would distinguish this project from the typical minor park improvement projects that are categorically exempt under Sections 15301, 15303, and 15304. The scale, scope, and location of the project are consistent with the intent of these exemptions, and there is no evidence of a reasonable possibility of a significant effect on the environment due to unusual circumstances.

For these reasons, the project is appropriately found to be categorically exempt from CEQA, and no further environmental review is required.
